request.question: When did Limp Bizkit become a rap rock band?

title: Three Dollar Bill, Y'all
Limp Bizkit
Icon (Limp Bizkit album)
Eat You Alive
Ready to Go (Limp Bizkit song)
Results May Vary
Limp Bizkit discography
Significant Other
Stampede of the Disco Elephants
Home Sweet Home/Bittersweet Symphony
sentences: Three Dollar Bill, Y'all$ is the debut studio album by American rap rock band Limp Bizkit.

Released on July 1, 1997, the album established the band's trademark sound with the singles "Counterfeit", which was influenced by hip hop and heavy metal, and "Faith", a cover of a song by George Michael.
Limp Bizkit's rearrangement of the song incorporated heavier guitar playing by Wes Borland and scratching by DJ Lethal.
Limp Bizkit is an American rap rock band from Jacksonville, Florida, formed in 1994.
Their lineup consists of Fred Durst (lead vocals), Sam Rivers (bass, backing vocals), John Otto (drums, percussion), and Wes Borland (guitars, backing vocals).
Their music is marked by Durst's angry vocal delivery and Borland's sonic experimentation.
Borland's elaborate visual appearance, which includes face and body paint, masks and uniforms, also plays a large role in the band's elaborate live shows.
The band has been nominated for three Grammy Awards, have sold 40 million records worldwide and won several other awards.
Icon is the fourth compilation album by American rock band Limp Bizkit.

Released in 2011, it is a retrospective compiling material from the band's albums "Three Dollar Bill, Yall$", "Significant Other", "Chocolate Starfish and the Hot Dog Flavored Water", "Results May Vary" and "The Unquestionable Truth (Part 1)".
"Eat You Alive" is a song by the band Limp Bizkit.
It was released in September 2003 as a single from their fourth studio album "Results May Vary" (2003).
The song was written by Fred Durst, John Otto, Sam Rivers and Mike Smith, and is Limp Bizkit's first single without Wes Borland, who had left the band in 2001.
"Ready to Go" is a song by the American nu metal band Limp Bizkit.
The single features rapper and then label-mate Lil Wayne and is produced by Polow Da Don.
The single is Limp Bizkit's first release for Cash Money Records after their departure from Interscope in 2011.
The song was released in March 2013 as a free download on the band's website and, on April 16, as a digital single on iTunes and Amazon.
Results May Vary is the fourth studio album by the American rap rock band Limp Bizkit.
Released on September 23, 2003, it is the band's only release under the sole-leadership of vocalist Fred Durst after the temporary departure of guitarist Wes Borland, who left the band in 2001.

Snot guitarist Mike Smith was initially brought in to replace Borland, although the band's falling-out with Smith later lead to his departure, with much of the material recorded with him being discarded from the final release.
Durst and a number of guests ended up handling the majority of the album's guitar work.

The discography of Limp Bizkit, an American nu metal band, consists of five studio albums, three compilation albums, one remix album, one live album, one extended play, 26 singles, three promotional singles, 27 music videos and two video albums.
Limp Bizkit formed in 1995 in Jacksonville, Florida.
The band has sold an estimated 50 million albums worldwide.
Significant Other is the second album by American rap rock band Limp Bizkit.
Released in 1999 by Flip/Interscope Records, the album saw the band expanding its sound from that of its debut album "Three Dollar Bill, Yall", to incorporate further metal and hip hop influences.
"Significant Other" was co-produced by Terry Date and Limp Bizkit.
The album has sold at least 16 million copies worldwide.
Stampede of the Disco Elephants is the upcoming sixth studio album by American nu metal band Limp Bizkit.
It is set to be Limp Bizkit's first album to be released since DJ Lethal's departure in May 2012.
After the original line-up reunited in 2009, "Gold Cobra" was released in 2011.
The band left Interscope and signed up with Cash Money Records in February 2012.
The first single "Lightz" was released on October 26, 2012.
After many delays, the second single for the album, "Ready to Go", featuring label mate Lil Wayne was released on April 16, 2013, to positive reviews.
It is the first album since "The Unquestionable Truth (Part 1)" to be produced by Ross Robinson.
"Home Sweet Home / Bittersweet Symphony", also known as "Bittersweet Home" is a song by American rock group Limp Bizkit.
It was released in December 2005 as the only single from the compilation album "Greatest Hitz".
The song is a medley of "Home Sweet Home" by Mötley Crüe and "Bitter Sweet Symphony" by The Verve.

It is, along with the songs "Why" and "Lean On Me", the only new content on the "Greatest Hitz" compilation, and is the third cover song Limp Bizkit has released on an album (the others being The Who's "Behind Blue Eyes", and George Michael's "Faith").

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

Released in 1999 by Flip/Interscope Records, the album saw the band expanding its sound from that of its debut album "Three Dollar Bill, Yall", to incorporate further metal and hip hop influences.  "Significant Other" was co-produced by Terry Date and Limp Bizkit.  The album has sold at least 16 million copies worldwide.

Limp Bizkit | Limp Bizkit is an American rap rock band from Jacksonville, Florida, formed in 1994.  Their lineup consists of Fred Durst (lead vocals), Sam Rivers (bass, backing vocals), John Otto (drums, percussion), and Wes Borland (guitars, backing vocals).  Their music is marked by Durst's angry vocal delivery and Borland's sonic experimentation.  Borland's elaborate visual appearance, which includes face and body paint, masks and uniforms, also plays a large role in the band's elaborate live shows.  The band has been nominated for three Grammy Awards, have sold 40 million records worldwide and won several other awards.

Released in 2005, it is a retrospective compiling material from the band's albums "Three Dollar Bill, Yall$", "Significant Other", "Chocolate Starfish and the Hot Dog Flavored Water" and "Results May Vary".

Icon (Limp Bizkit album) | Icon is the fourth compilation album by American rock band Limp Bizkit.  Released in 2011, it is a retrospective compiling material from the band's albums "Three Dollar Bill, Yall$", "Significant Other", "Chocolate Starfish and the Hot Dog Flavored Water", "Results May Vary" and "The Unquestionable Truth (Part 1)".

Collected (Limp Bizkit album) | Collected is the third compilation album by American rock band Limp Bizkit.  Released in 2008, it a retrospective compilation album from the band's albums "Three Dollar Bill, Yall$", "Significant Other", "Chocolate Starfish and the Hot Dog Flavored Water", "Results May Vary" and "The Unquestionable Truth (Part 1)".

Limp Bizkit formed in 1995 in Jacksonville, Florida.  The band has sold an estimated 50 million albums worldwide.
response.summary_1: Passages call Limp Bizkit "an American rap rock band" and note their debut album "Three Dollar Bill, Y'all" was "Released on July 1, 1997" (evidence anchor: "Three Dollar Bill, Y'all is the debut studio album by American rap rock band Limp Bizkit. Released on July 1, 1997"). However, formation-year statements conflict: one passage says "formed in 1994" (evidence anchor: "Limp Bizkit ... formed in 1994") while another says "Limp Bizkit formed in 1995 in Jacksonville, Florida" (evidence anchor: "Limp Bizkit formed in 1995 in Jacksonville, Florida"). Missing/ambiguous fact for the next hop: the authoritative formation year or the earliest source that labels them a "rap rock" band. Retrieval direction: fetch an authoritative band history (official site, reputable bios, or early press/interviews) to confirm the band's formation year and/or when they began being described as rap rock.
response.hop2_query: Limp Bizkit formation year 1994 1995 official biography AllMusic Rolling Stone interview earliest rap rock label
hop2_docs: Limp Bizkit | Limp Bizkit is an American rap rock band from Jacksonville, Florida, formed in 1994.  Their lineup consists of Fred Durst (lead vocals), Sam Rivers (bass, backing vocals), John Otto (drums, percussion), and Wes Borland (guitars, backing vocals).  Their music is marked by Durst's angry vocal delivery and Borland's sonic experimentation.  Borland's elaborate visual appearance, which includes face and body paint, masks and uniforms, also plays a large role in the band's elaborate live shows.  The band has been nominated for three Grammy Awards, have sold 40 million records worldwide and won several other awards.

Three Dollar Bill, Y'all | Three Dollar Bill, Y'all$ is the debut studio album by American rap rock band Limp Bizkit.  Released on July 1, 1997, the album established the band's trademark sound with the singles "Counterfeit", which was influenced by hip hop and heavy metal, and "Faith", a cover of a song by George Michael.  Limp Bizkit's rearrangement of the song incorporated heavier guitar playing by Wes Borland and scratching by DJ Lethal.

Significant Other | Significant Other is the second album by American rap rock band Limp Bizkit.  Released in 1999 by Flip/Interscope Records, the album saw the band expanding its sound from that of its debut album "Three Dollar Bill, Yall", to incorporate further metal and hip hop influences.  "Significant Other" was co-produced by Terry Date and Limp Bizkit.  The album has sold at least 16 million copies worldwide.

Ready to Go (Limp Bizkit song) | "Ready to Go" is a song by the American nu metal band Limp Bizkit.  The single features rapper and then label-mate Lil Wayne and is produced by Polow Da Don.  The single is Limp Bizkit's first release for Cash Money Records after their departure from Interscope in 2011.  The song was released in March 2013 as a free download on the band's website and, on April 16, as a digital single on iTunes and Amazon.

Limp Bizkit discography | The discography of Limp Bizkit, an American nu metal band, consists of five studio albums, three compilation albums, one remix album, one live album, one extended play, 26 singles, three promotional singles, 27 music videos and two video albums.  Limp Bizkit formed in 1995 in Jacksonville, Florida.  The band has sold an estimated 50 million albums worldwide.

Flip Records (1994) | Flip Records is a Californian record label, started by Jordan Schur in 1994.  Flip had distribution deals with other labels including Epic, Elektra and A&M until Schur was named president of Geffen and issued through Geffen/Interscope.  The label is known for the signing of popular nu metal bands such as Limp Bizkit, Staind, Dope and Cold.

Anger Management Tour | The inaugural Anger Management Tour was founded and started in the fall 2000 by Limp Bizkit, Papa Roach, and after the release of "The Marshall Mathers LP" by Eminem.  Originally targeted at both rap and rock fans, the first "Anger Management" outing took place in 2000.  In the process of starting the large tour, Eminem wanted to include Nine Inch Nails into the list of artists, but Marilyn Manson joining the tour would raise the issues between him and Trent Reznor.  Eminem decided to not ask Reznor to join the tour due to the friendship he has with Manson.  Eminem also came up with the name for the tour, being quoted in "Rolling Stone" saying "It's the perfect name for a tour like this, because most of the music featured in the tour is about anger, frustration, and the bullshit of our lives and the fans loving every minute of it.  It's the blend of hardcore rap, industrial metal, and alternative metal that gives it the anger management feel, literally."
response.summary_2: Limp Bizkit is described in the passages as an American rap rock/nu metal band, and their debut album Three Dollar Bill, Y'all was released July 1, 1997. Sources conflict on the band's formation year: one passage says they "formed in 1994" while another says they "formed in 1995 in Jacksonville, Florida."
